title: artemisia capillaris
intro: Artemisia capillaris, also known as Yin Chen Hao, is a type of flowering plant belonging to the daisy family that is native to East Asia. It has been used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects. The health benefits of Artemisia capillaris include improving liver function, reducing fever, and aiding in digestion, as well as exhibiting potential anti-cancer and anti-aging properties. In herbal preparations, Artemisia capillaris is often combined with other herbs and used in teas, infusions, and tinctures to take advantage of its medicinal properties.
intro_study: According to Biological & pharmaceutical bulletin, Artemisia capillaris has distinct medicinal purposes from 'Aeyup', which are used as important medicinal material in traditional Korean medicine, suggesting that it may be used for other purposes.
benefits_desc: Artemisia capillaris is used to alleviate various health issues, including reducing inflammation and improving cardiovascular health. Its medicinal properties have been shown to effectively lower blood pressure, prevent the formation of blood clots, and even help in the management of diabetes. This plant is also known to have antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, which can aid in preventing the growth of cancer cells and protecting against various infections. Regular consumption of Artemisia capillaris has also been linked to improved digestion, reduced risk of certain neurological disorders, and enhanced overall immune function.

benefits_list.name: antioxidant properties
benefits_list.desc: artemisia capillaris has antioxidant properties that help protect the body from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals.

benefits_list.desc: the plant has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with conditions such as arthritis and other inflammatory diseases.

benefits_list.desc: artemisia capillaris has antimicrobial properties that help combat bacterial, fungal, and viral infections, promoting overall health and well-being.

benefits_list.desc: the plant has been shown to have antidiabetic effects, helping to regulate blood sugar levels and improve insulin sensitivity.

benefits_list.name: digestive health
benefits_list.desc: the plant has been used to aid digestion and relieve symptoms associated with digestive disorders, such as bloating, cramps, and diarrhea.

benefits_list.name: neuroprotective effects
benefits_list.desc: artemisia capillaris has been shown to have neuroprotective effects, helping to prevent or slow the progression of neurodegenerative diseases such as alzheimer's and parkinson's.

actions_desc: Artemisia capillaris has been found to exhibit a range of therapeutic actions, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antibacterial, antifungal, and antiviral properties. These actions affect various body systems, such as the cardiovascular system, where it may help to regulate blood pressure and improve circulation, as well as the digestive system, where it may aid in the treatment of gastrointestinal issues and improve nutrient absorption. Additionally, Artemisia capillaris has demonstrated nervine, adaptogenic, and hepatoprotective properties, which can influence the nervous system, endocrine system, and liver function, respectively. Its anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory actions also affect the immune system, where it may help to regulate immune responses and reduce inflammation.

constituents_desc: Artemisia capillaris has been found to contain a rich array of bioactive constituents, including flavonoids such as apigenin and luteolin, which possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant also contains tannins, like gallic acid and ellagic acid, which exhibit antimicrobial and antiviral activities. Additionally, Artemisia capillaris is rich in phenolic acids, including ferulic acid and caffeic acid, which have been shown to have anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory effects. Furthermore, the plant contains lignans, such as sesamin and sesamolin, which have been found to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, as well as terpenoids, like artesunic acid, which possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities.

parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Artemisia capillaris are the leaves and roots, which have been employed in traditional medicine for various purposes. The leaves are used to treat fever, flu, and respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, due to their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The roots, on the other hand, are used as a diuretic to relieve edema and hypertension, and also as an anti-inflammatory to treat arthritis and other joint-related conditions. Furthermore, both the leaves and roots are also used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and constipation, due to their ability to regulate gut health and prevent the growth of pathogens.

preparations_desc: Artemisia capillaris most common herbal preparations are infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which have been widely used for their medicinal properties. Infusions, made by steeping the dried flowers and leaves in hot water, are often used to relieve stress and anxiety, improve sleep quality, and boost the immune system. Decoctions, made by boiling the roots and stems in water, are commonly used to treat digestive issues, such as constipation, diarrhea, and nausea, as well as to reduce fever and alleviate respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and asthma. Additionally, tinctures, made by soaking the plant in a solvent, are often used to treat inflammatory conditions, such as arthritis and gout, and to support liver and kidney health.

side_effects_desc: Artemisia capillaris side effects are generally mild but can be severe in some individuals, particularly those with pre-existing liver or kidney conditions. These side effects include dizziness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach pain, headaches, insomnia, allergic reactions, and respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma exacerbation. People who are most likely to experience these side effects are those who take high doses of the plant's extract, use it for extended periods, or combine it with other medications like blood thinners, diabetes medications, or sedatives. To minimize the risk of side effects, individuals should avoid using Artemisia capillaris if they have a history of bleeding disorders, take medications that interact with it, or are pregnant or breastfeeding.

side_effects_list.name: nausea
side_effects_list.desc: feeling queasy or experiencing stomach discomfort, often accompanied by vomiting.

side_effects_list.name: dizziness
side_effects_list.desc: a feeling of lightheadedness or unsteadiness, which may be accompanied by vertigo.

side_effects_list.name: allergic reactions
side_effects_list.desc: an overreaction of the immune system to the plant's compounds, which can cause symptoms such as hives, itching, or swelling.

side_effects_list.name: headaches
side_effects_list.desc: a type of pain or discomfort in the head, often described as throbbing, sharp, or dull.

side_effects_list.name: skin irritation
side_effects_list.desc: redness, itching, or inflammation of the skin, often caused by contact with the plant's oils or sap.

side_effects_list.name: abdominal pain
side_effects_list.desc: a feeling of discomfort or pain in the abdominal region, which can be sharp, dull, or crampy.

side_effects_list.name: vomiting
side_effects_list.desc: the forceful expulsion of stomach contents through the mouth, often caused by nausea or other gastrointestinal issues.
